did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically GhostNet (V1, V2, V3) convolutional neural networks, for the binary classification of dermoscopic images (malignant vs. benign) using the ISIC-2019 dataset. The study involves training, fine-tuning, and evaluating these AI models to achieve dermatologist-level sensitivity for skin cancer detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the training and fine-tuning of GhostNet variants (V1, V2, V3) on the ISIC-2019 dataset using SMOTE-ENN for class imbalance. The study reports specific quantitative results (e.g., 95% accuracy, 0.99 AUC) and compares them against baselines, which characterizes it as an original technical study rather than a review.

title: A deep learning approach powered by GhostNet for skin cancer classification
journal: Gümüşhane Üniversitesi Fen Bilimleri Enstitüsü Dergisi
brief_summary: This study developed an efficient deep learning system for the binary classification of dermoscopic images into malignant and benign categories using the GhostNet family of convolutional neural networks. The researchers utilized the publicly available ISIC-2019 dataset, addressing extreme class imbalance through a hybrid SMOTE-ENN technique during training. Three GhostNet variants (V1, V2, and V3) were end-to-end fine-tuned with identical preprocessing and augmentation settings to ensure fair comparison. The evaluation focused on accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC, with GhostNet V2 augmented by depth-wise attention achieving the strongest performance. Key results included 95% accuracy, 94% malignant class recall, an F1-score of 94.3%, and an AUC of 0.99. The authors concluded that GhostNet V2 offers a viable, lightweight solution for dermatologist-level sensitivity suitable for real-time, resource-constrained point-of-care settings.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (malignant vs. benign skin lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly performs binary classification of dermoscopic images into malignant and benign categories using the ISIC-2019 dataset. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic labels rather than continuous values. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC, which are standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A publicly available benchmark of dermoscopic images released by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) as part of the 2019 ISIC Challenge. It contains diverse dermoscopic images labeled based on expert dermatological diagnoses.

use.training: Used for training GhostNet V1, V2, and V3 models. The training subset consisted of 20,264 images. Class imbalance was addressed using SMOTE-ENN applied to the training set embeddings.
use.validation: Used for validation during training (early stopping based on validation accuracy). The paper states a split of 20% for validation/test combined, but does not provide a specific count for validation alone.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation on a held-out test set of 2,922 dermoscopic images (with equal numbers of benign and malignant cases).

statistical_methods_used.method: SMOTE-ENN (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique coupled with Edited Nearest Neighbours)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Addressing extreme class imbalance in the training dataset by generating synthetic samples for the minority class and cleaning the majority class.
Ensuring representative distribution of classes in the training and validation/test sets.
Examining error types (true positives, false positives, true negatives, false negatives) and calculating sensitivity (recall) and specificity on the test set.
Evaluating discriminative power across different classification thresholds and comparing models (V1, V2, V3) via Area Under the Curve (AUC).
Comparing GhostNet V1, V2, and V3 performance using identical preprocessing, augmentation, hyperparameters, and training conditions to ensure validity of the comparison.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model comparisons were based on descriptive performance metrics (accuracy, recall, F1-score, AUC) and qualitative analysis of ROC curves and confusion matrices.

ISIC 2019.split: Binary classification (Malignant vs. Benign) on a held-out test set of 2,922 images (1,498 benign, 1,424 malignant)

GhostNet V2 (with depth-wise attention).Confusion Matrix Details: 1,337 true positives (malignant), 87 false negatives (malignant); 1,427 true negatives (benign), 71 false positives (benign)

ISIC 2019.notes: GhostNet V2 outperformed V1 and V3. V1 and V3 had identical performance metrics in this study. The study notes that V2's performance is attributed to DFC attention modules. Inference time was <30 ms per image on mobile-class hardware.

key_components.GhostNet V1: Ghost modules (intrinsic feature maps + cheap linear operations)
key_components.GhostNet V3: Training recipe (re-parameterization, knowledge distillation, schedule/augmentation tuning)

training_details.hardware: Lenovo gaming PC with AMD Ryzen 7 processor and NVIDIA GeForce RTX GPU

classification_or_output_head.details: Final layer swapped with a binary classification layer for malignant vs. benign classification

preprocessing_techniques: SMOTE-ENN (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique coupled with Edited Nearest Neighbours) applied to training set embeddings to address class imbalance

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training with a scale range of 0.85–1.0.
Applied during training with brightness/contrast/saturation set to 0.10 and hue set to 0.05.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: SMOTE-ENN (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique coupled with Edited Nearest Neighbours)
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used for class balancing augmentation on the training set embeddings (GhostNetV2 feature space) to address extreme class imbalance. Applied only to the training set; validation and test sets remained unchanged. Resulted in an approximately 1:1 class ratio (10,883 benign and 10,883 malignant images) after balancing.

conclusions: GhostNet V2, augmented with depth-wise attention, achieved the strongest performance among the tested variants, yielding 95% accuracy, 94% malignant class recall (sensitivity), an F1-score of 94.3%, and an AUC of 0.99.

GhostNet V2 outperformed GhostNet V1 and V3, which both achieved 91% accuracy and 0.97 AUC, demonstrating the benefit of the Decoupled Fully-Connected (DFC) attention mechanism for capturing global structural cues in melanoma-vs-benign discrimination.
The study confirms that efficient architectures like GhostNet can achieve dermatologist-level sensitivity for skin cancer classification without the high computational requirements of heavier models.
GhostNet V2 is feasible for real-time inference on resource-constrained mobile hardware, with an inference time of less than 30 ms per image and a parameter count of 6.1 million.
The use of hybrid SMOTE-ENN to correct extreme class imbalance in the ISIC-2019 dataset was effective in enabling the models to achieve high sensitivity for the malignant class.

GhostNet V3 did not outperform V2 in this specific setup, likely because the study did not employ the full V3 training recipe (such as an external teacher model or specific re-parameterization branches) required to realize its potential gains.
The proposed system is a viable candidate for point-of-care or resource-limited clinical settings due to its balance of high diagnostic performance and low computational cost.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC-2019 dataset, which is a public benchmark; results may not generalize to external clinical populations or different imaging devices without external validation.

Class imbalance was addressed using SMOTE-ENN applied only to the training set embeddings (feature space), not pixel space; this avoids generating artificial pixels but introduces synthetic data that may not reflect true biological variance.

The test set was constructed with an equal number of benign and malignant cases (1,498 benign, 1,424 malignant), which does not reflect the natural prevalence of skin cancer in clinical practice and may overestimate positive predictive value.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for performance comparisons between GhostNet variants or against baselines.

GhostNet V3 did not outperform V2 in this study, likely because the full V3 training recipe (e.g., re-parameterization, knowledge distillation) was not fully implemented, limiting the assessment of V3's potential.

The authors acknowledge that ISIC metrics vary by task, split, and preprocessing; while they document their split, comparisons with other studies in Table 4 are complicated by differing evaluation protocols (e.g., 8-class vs. binary, official vs. custom splits).
The study is a follow-up to prior work by the same authors (Almasani & Ture, 2025), raising potential concerns about self-reinforcing validation or lack of independent replication by other groups.
Inference time was reported as <30 ms on a specific mobile-class hardware setup, but real-world deployment performance may vary significantly across different mobile devices and operating systems.
